FT_IC1.evidence: Uses baseline data from ABCD Study (10,842 participants); explicitly states 'Baseline data from 10,842 participants in the Adolescent Brain Cognition Development Study'

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying SM: social media visiting (Facebook, Instagram, video-chatting) separately measured; qualifying smartphone: video games, texting separately measured. B1 applies: YSTS with separate β for each activity (passive viewing, video games, social media sites/video chat, texting). All reported as distinct per-item coefficients.

FT_IC3.evidence: Poisson regression (generalized linear models with generalized estimation equations) comparing screen time by autism status and gender; prevalence ratios with 95% CI reported

FT_IC4.evidence: Tables 2-5 report ratio of means (95% CI), prevalence ratios (95% CI), and p-values. Example: Table 2 shows boys with autism 2.9h vs 2.3h TV (p<0.001); girls with autism 64.4 vs 37.9 min video games (p=0.03)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis comparing screen time patterns by autism status and gender; propensity score matched analysis

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article; no evidence of preprint/abstract duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SM is not the dependent variable in all analyses; screen time is predictor of obesity (obesity is outcome in Tables 3-5). Analysis includes non-demographic predictors (autism status) and outcome relationships that violate EC7 condition (a). Not a demographic→SM longitudinal analysis. B18 carve-out does not apply; SM not dual-role.
notes: Strong ABCD Study paper with detailed screen time breakdowns. Propensity score matching employed for autism status. Gender-stratified analyses included.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes 8,324 children (9-11 years) from ABCD Study baseline and year 2 follow-up (2016-2018 baseline, 2018-2020 year 2)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone: 'daily screen time' measured as Screen-Activity Proportion (SAP). B1 applies: reporting separate screen time exposure (qualified as smartphone variable) with distinct coefficient in models predicting inhibitory control network

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation analysis (Pearson r with bootstrap 50,000 resampling); mediation analysis (bootstrapping); Bayesian posterior inference; group-level t-tests comparing high vs low SAP groups

FT_IC4.evidence: Correlation coefficients (r), 95% bootstrap CI, BF10 (Bayes factors), t-statistics, p-values reported. Figure 1 and text report r=-0.040 (95% CI [-0.0605, -0.0186]), BF10=13.10; year 2: r=-0.0933 (95% CI [-0.1231, -0.0635]), BF10=428995.74; mediation indirect effect: B=-6.920e-5, p<0.001

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD Study analysis examining association between screen time and inhibitory control network development over 2-year period

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Developmental Cognitive Neuroscience (2023); no duplicate evidence

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable predicting neurobiological outcome (fronto-striatal connectivity), not dependent variable. Analysis includes non-demographic predictors (reward sensitivity/BAS scores) and outcome relationships. Not demographic-only→SM framework. B18/B20 not applicable.
notes: Longitudinal fMRI connectivity study with robust statistical approaches (Bayesian + Bootstrap). Screen time (SAP) measured as proportion relative to activity time. Mediation analysis shows SAP mediates BAS→IC network relationship.

FT_IC1.evidence: Analyzes prospective cohort data from ABCD Study; baseline 2016-2018, two-year follow-up 2018-2020; N=11,633 at baseline

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone: texting, video chatting, watching videos, playing video games all separately measured. Qualifying SM: social media separately reported. B1 applies: multiple distinct screen modalities reported with separate coefficients (aOR for texting, video chat, videos, video games all reported separately)

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression examining prospective associations between baseline screen time and suicidal behaviors at 2-year follow-up; multiple logistic regression models adjusted for covariates

FT_IC4.evidence: Adjusted odds ratios (aOR) with 95% CI reported for all screen time modalities. Total screen time: aOR 1.09 (95% CI 1.03-1.14); texting: aOR 1.36 (95% CI 1.06-1.74); video chatting: aOR 1.30 (95% CI 1.03-1.65); videos: aOR 1.21 (95% CI 1.04-1.39); video games: aOR 1.18 (95% CI 1.01-1.38)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective ABCD Study analysis linking baseline screen time to suicidal behaviors at 2-year follow-up using KSADS-5 assessment

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Preventive Medicine (2023); no evidence of duplicate preprint/abstract

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (exposure) predicting suicidal behaviors (outcome), not dependent variable. Non-demographic confounders included (baseline major depressive disorder, parental psychopathology, family history). Not demographic-only→SM framework. B18/B20 not applicable.
notes: Large prospective cohort study with 1.38% reporting suicidal behaviors at follow-up. Sensitivity analyses excluding baseline suicidal behaviors reported. Specific screen modalities most strongly associated: texting, video chat, videos, video games.
